subject: Re: Suggestion for API Rate Limiting
body:
Dear John,

Thank you for reaching out and sharing your valuable suggestion! We appreciate you taking the time to help us improve our services.

We understand the importance of rate limiting for API security and performance. We'll forward your suggestion to our development team for consideration in future updates.

While we can't promise immediate implementation, we want to assure you that we value your feedback and are committed to providing the best possible API development solutions.

Best regards,
The Aetheros Support Team

Moreover, we should offer our employees the chance to practice and apply their new competencies. I consider this update essential for the company's future and hope you will take my request into consideration.

07.18.2002 GnomeREPORT Keep your Web site working fast and smart with UltraDNS. More reliableperformance means more traffic, more sales, and more profits. Outsource yourDNS management and save. Basic DNS for only $1.00 a month. Click here tofind out more.I thought I baked my Dell PowerEdge 1400SC server yesterday when it refused to boot completely. I borrowed a data vac from a friend and decided to clean all of the dust and cat hair from my systems, then after reconnecting everything, the server would boot to the initial splash screen, where there is a POST progress bar, but would not proceed past past about the first 1/5th of the bar. Uh-oh. What did I fry?As it turns out, it was another case of "always check the small stuff". Oh, I had all of the cables plugged back in to the server properly, I checked all of that, but it didn't dawn on me until about a half dozen boot attempts later that I should probably turn an eye toward the other end of the cables. That server is connected to an 8-port Compaq KVM switch and with all of the yanking, plucking and shuffling, the keyboard cable had been eeked out of the switch just a tad. Why the server didn't display a keyboard warning message like every other computer on the planet, I can only attribute to the BIOS developers. Server Satchel, running Red Hat Linux 7.2 is now operating as it was previously. The penguin is happy. The server case is even black. How appropriate...See You in August,
Randy Nieland
GnomeNETWORKWindows XP Security Checklist
Finely crafted by Bernie Klinder all of the security features crammed into Windows XP, where does one start? Do you need VPN capability? Is NTFS an appropriate file system choice for you? Is there any value at all in Simple File Sharing? After much hounding, Bernie Klinder has drawn up a healthy checklist of things you need to address under Windows XP in order to maintain a system that is both security and compatible with the rest of your computing world.
GnomeSYSTEMATI Still Makes Video Cards? it isn't so! Yes, apparently they do still have something to say about NVIDIA's dominance in the video card market. Seeing as how they've been all but dumped by the majority of top-tier system makers, it's hard to believe that they are capable of a serious comeback. Judging from what AnandTech has to say about the forthcoming R300-based cards, it appears that they haven't thrown in the towel just yet. I'm all for competition, so I welcome the effective re-entrance into the high end market. Let's all hope that their driver development has improved as much as their hardware seems to have. GET WINDOWS XP TIPS: Months after the release of Windows XP Professional and Home editions, people are still learning the nuances of Microsoft's newest operating system. Whether you've been using it for a while or are planning to upgrade to the new OS soon, this ebook is right up your alley. GET YOUR COPY of Lockergnome's Top 50 Windows XP tips now!
GnomeALERTZToday's Top 5 kbAlertzQ292108 - WD2002: GetSpellingSuggestions Method Does Not List Non-English Language Suggestions (Q292108)Q290981 - WD2002: "File Conversion Encoding" Dialog Box Appears When You Open Text File (Q290981)Q290969 - WD2002: Run-Time Error 5 When You Use Mid(), Left(), or Right() Function (Q290969)Q290967 - WD2002: Cannot Place a Bullet in the Middle of a Sentence or Paragraph (Q290967)Q201953 - WD2000: Macro to Find Automatic (Soft) Page Breaks (Q201953) Recommend It!
GnomeASSISTANTTechnical Censorship I liked that cup of Mocha (spelled M-O-C-H-A and I'll explain in a minute) I drank at Starbucks when Jake and I visited last week, but if I wanted to relay that to someone using a Yahoo email account, you might see an alternate word. Thus the additional hyphenated spelling just in case those of you using Yahoo to subscribe to Lockergnome actually received your issues and the word has been altered to suit their personal opinion of my choice in drinks. They've also had a terrible time interpretting our W3C standardized HTML lately, dropping in copious #message references throughout the text in their new Beta email client. So why the text replacement? It's another filtering technique to stop certain words that might relate to JavaScript functions. I guess I'll have to choose another beverage next time. Silly me.
GnomeSTUFFYer Kidding... Really? all sorts of sarcastic today, aren't I? Those of us that have had to manage budgets are used to dealing with math in terms of hardware, software, maintenance and personnel costs. It's not really that difficult. It's certainly not hard to see that dropping a free version of Linux on a server with less hardware requirements just to do some file sharing, printing and maybe an Intranet page or three is far cheaper than using Windows. At very long last, Microsoft has admitted as much. Of course, they're quick to point out that they have much more to offer than Linux, which may indeed be true. But at the end of the day, when you compare donuts to donuts and like functions against one another, Linux is cheaper. For a shop of Windows-trained staff, dealing with Linux will certainly be a challenge, requiring additional training, suffer the odd blunder here and there, and be less forgiving when it comes to detailed configuration. On the same token, make a Linux whiz use Windows and see how quickly you'll be searching for a replacement. Cheap is in the budget of the beholder.Recommend It!

subject: Re: Inquiry about API Monitoring and IAM integration
body:
Hi John,

Thank you for your interest in our API monitoring and IAM services.

We're happy to confirm that both services are tightly integrated to offer the functionalities you described.

1. **Automated Alerts based on IAM Events:** You can configure custom alerts within the API monitoring platform to be triggered by specific IAM events, including role changes, user creation, or permission modifications. Our documentation provides detailed steps on setting up these alerts: [link to documentation]

2. **Role-Based Access Control for API Monitoring:** You can leverage the roles and permissions defined within the IAM service to control access to API monitoring dashboards and metrics. This ensures that only authorized users can view sensitive information.

For a more in-depth understanding, we recommend exploring our API documentation, which provides detailed information on the integration points between these services: [link to API documentation]

Please let us know if you have any further questions. We're here to help!

Best regards,
Aetheros Support

subject: Sticky summer for inflation
body: Plus, China’s demographic decline and Hillary Clinton in conversation ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ ‌ All newsletters | Read in browser Roula Khalaf Editor ‌ ‌ May 25 2023 Hello from London, where the newsroom has been reporting on rather bad economic news: UK inflation fell to 8.7 per cent in April, according to the Office for National Statistics — a much smaller drop than the Bank of England expected. UK bond markets jolted at the news, with gilt yields nearing the levels of last year’s “mini” Budget crisis. Inflation in the UK is now about double the equivalent US rate, and well above almost all of western Europe. The return of high inflation and slow growth is reminiscent of the 1970s. The FT’s economics editor Chris Giles is therefore asking: Is the UK once again the ‘sick man’ of Europe?Traders predict more trouble is ahead for UK bonds, after stronger than expected inflation data yesterday © Reuters My choices this week ‌ ‌ US legislators have been drawing up rules that affect their personal investments because of antiquated loopholes in conflict of interest laws. This excellent FT analysis exposes how lawmakers could be benefiting from Capitol Hill’s failure to keep pace with a transformed investment sector.Russia is fortifying its frontline in anticipation of the long-awaited Ukrainian counter-offensive. By analysing satellite imagery and mapping changes on the ground, our correspondent in Kyiv explores how the attack might play out. (free to read)“Forever chemicals” that are vital to the production of microchips could have life-threatening health and environmental effects, growing evidence suggests. Facing calls for more stringent regulation, how is a rapidly growing industry responding to the threat?(If you’d like to read this article for free, please download our iPhone app, FT Edit, where it is in today’s edition. You don’t need to register or give payment details, you can simply download and start reading FT Edit, and the first month is free.)The G7 heads of government painted a picture of unipolar economic dominance at their summit in Hiroshima last weekend. But despite its best efforts, the G7 cannot run the world, writes the FT’s chief economics commentator Martin Wolf.China’s rapidly ageing population — in part a legacy of its one-child policy — is forcing the government to make painful reforms tackling pensions, healthcare and the workforce. This poignant report from Rudong, China’s oldest county, offers a snapshot of China’s demographic decline. (free to read)Last weekend I was in Washington DC for a brilliant day at the FT Weekend festival. Among other highlights, the 2016 Democratic presidential candidate Hillary Clinton discussed China, Putin, Trump and Netflix series The Diplomat, with the FT’s US national editor Ed Luce. Watch the interview on ft.com here.Thanks for readingRoula PS Don’t forget our special subscription offer, which means you get a third off a Standard digital subscription to the Financial Times. This gives you access to ft.com as well as excellent newsletters including the award-winning Inside Politics, Working It and our flagship news briefing, FirstFT. ‌ ‌ Recommended newsletters for you In Today’s FT Your daily overview of FT’s top stories. Sign up MBA 101 Take our six-week course on how to get into business school. Register here ‌ Unsubscribe ‌ ‌ Terms & Conditions ‌ ‌ Privacy Policy ‌ ‌ Cookie Policy ‌ © THE FINANCIAL TIMES LTD 2023. All rights reserved. You have received this email because you have signed up from the Emails & Alerts page or by using a one-click sign-up link from either an FT email or via FT.com. This email was sent by a company owned by Financial Times Group Limited (FT Group), registered office at Bracken House, 1 Friday Street, London EC4M 9BT. Registered in England and Wales with company number 879531.

subject: Re: [opensuse] OpenSuse 11
body: Kai Ponte wrote: > On Thursday 07 February 2008 04:48:00 pm Wolfgang Woehl wrote: >> Freitag, 8. Februar 2008 Randall R Schulz: >>> That is manifestly false. It takes an explicit vulnerability for >>> this to happen. The classic one is unchecked overflow of a buffer >> Any given nasty application would need 1 system call to remove your >> homedir. Call that "unfair" or "vulnerability", whatever. >> >> How you would run into such a nasty app is another story. But isn't >> saying that you couldn't a bit over-optimistic? > > Okay: I've read enough of this tit-for-tat. > > Here's the fact: Any OS can be vulnerable to an attack. I've actually written > a sample buffer overflow that gained me (or rather my code) root access from > a non-privileged account right on my SUSE 9.3 system. (I got the code from > the February 2005 issue of Linux Magazine.) > > The difference between Linux and the legacy OS's like Wintendo is that the > buffer overflow would have a very difficult time spreading in the wild. IIRC, > there was a virus released for *nix in '97 or '98 but it quickly perished. > > And as I recall, the virus writer used it to patch the very same vulnerability which allowed it to work in the first place.
